<span class="meta-gravatar"><img alt="" src="https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/a950e9ac377878b6a53b406f5cb759fdb75f3e44610f6ee9c1350ee3e3ab4bba?s=96&d=monsterid&r=g" class="posted-on"><time class="updated" datetime="2023-12-12T20:23:48-08:00" itemprop="dateModified">December 12, 2023</time><time class="entry-date published" datetime="2023-11-21T19:37:38-08:00" itemprop="datePublished">November 21, 2023</time></span> </span></div></header><div class="entry-content" itemprop="text"><p>A year ago, I was firmly in the “I hate AI content” camp and it wasn’t until a few months into 2023 that I accepted that it was time for me to change my views.</p><p>My undergrad degree is in English and Journalism, so it’s easy to see why that was my initial view.</p><p>Truthfully, I still don’t love AI content. But it doesn’t really matter how I feel about it.</p><p>Because SEO and growing niche sites doesn’t have shit to do with emotions or feelings.</p><p>It’s about being willing to adapt and pivot to what’s working. So, I pivoted.</p><figure class="wp-block-table aligncenter"><table><tbody><tr><td><img decoding="async" src="https://embed.filekitcdn.com/e/4Kyb8QGruNwCP1tSgH7zuL/g7XawqBh6U36VmUvJysfj" width="498" height="auto" alt="g7XawqBh6U36VmUvJysfj" title="From Hating AI Content To A $1,000+/mo Site (Bulk AI Content Case Study) 1"></td></tr></tbody></table></figure><p><em>**And as always, I’m an affiliate marketer and this post contains affiliate links, meaning I will be compensated if you make a purchase after clicking on my link.</em></p><h2 class="wp-block-heading">How It Started</h2><p>I started out using CopyAI and Quillbot (just a little here and there) because I thought they were the best at the time. I continued testing every new AI tool though.</p><p>You may recall that I really hated <a href="https://koala.sh/?via=shawna" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Koala</a> when it was released, but after lots of improvements it’s one of the AI content tools that I use on the regular these days. (I also like <a href="https://www.cuppa.sh/?via=shawna" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Cuppa</a> and Zimmwriter)</p><p>But it still took me a while to really double down on AI content.</p><p>The real appeal for me is being able to get a draft published in just a few minutes compared to several hours.</p><p>I always work by getting a draft up first and then going back in and editing it to be better and to sprinkle in keywords that are popping up in Search Console.</p><p>Anyways, few months back, I started an experiment where I mass published a fuckload of AI content (using the cheaper 3.5 Turbo, not 4.0) to see what would happen.</p><p>The site in question has been live just over 1.5 years with only two posts published (written by me, not AI) before I started this little experiment with mass scale AI content  publishing.</p><h2 class="wp-block-heading">How It’s Been Going</h2><p>That AI test site continues to amaze me, while a site of mine that’s got great human-written content with unique research took an almost 80% hit in HCU.</p><p>Right now, this AI content-based site is monetized by display ads.</p><p>It’s earning almost $50/day. Can you fucking believe that?</p><p>But the best part is how little it cost me. (cause I spent like $30K last year on premium content for the site that got hit hard by HCU)</p><p>I opted to use Koala for this because I thought it would offer the best ROI in terms of time. I ordered the $2,000/mo plan, which gives you 10,000,000 words (that is a cost of $0.0002 per word).</p><p>BUT – I used a coupon code that gave me 50% off for that first month, making the per-word cost just $0.0001/word.</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>3,923,016 words published</li><li>at $0.0001 per word</li><li>= <strong>$392.30 total spend</strong></li></ul><p>If I’d paid the full price of $0.0002 per word, it would have cost me $784.60 all in.</p><p>Keep in mind that if I’d used <a href="https://www.cuppa.sh/?via=shawna" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cuppa</a> or Zimmwriter, then the cost probably would have been even less…but I think the time-saving aspect made Koala worth it.</p><p>The tl;dr is here is that the site is already profitable – and it would be profitable if I’d paid full price for that Koala package.</p><p>I first monetized the site with AdSense, but literally no one clicked on those ads.</p><p>Not a single fucking click.</p><p>So, zero dollars earned with that.</p><p>But after putting it on a premium ads network/partner, the money started rolling in.
